questions.question: A consumer has M = $72, Px = $6, Py = $3, and utility U(x,y) = x^0.5y^0.5. What bundle on the budget line maximizes utility, and what does it imply about the budget set?

choices.A: x = 6, y = 12; the optimum lies on the boundary because utility is increasing in both goods.
choices.B: x = 12, y = 0; the optimum is the x-intercept because x is more expensive.
choices.C: x = 4, y = 16; the optimum is inside the budget set because spending less is preferred.
choices.D: x = 6, y = 6; the optimum equalizes quantities.

questions.explanation: Equal Cobb-Douglas shares spend $36 on each good, so x = 6 and y = 12. With monotonic preferences, the optimum is on the budget line. B gives zero utility, C is not optimal with monotonic preferences, and D spends only $54.

questions.question: A consumer has $50 to spend on notebooks N at $5 and pens P at $2.50. Utility is U(N,P) = min(N,P), so the goods are perfect complements. What bundle maximizes utility?

choices.A: N = 10, P = 0; spend all money on notebooks.
choices.B: N = 0, P = 20; spend all money on pens.
choices.C: N = P = 6.67 approximately; choose the kink on the budget line where N equals P.
choices.D: N = 5, P = 10; spend equal dollars on both goods.

questions.explanation: Perfect complements require N = P. Then 5N + 2.5N = 50, so N = P = 6.67. A and B give zero utility, and D fails to match complements.

questions.question: A budget set is defined by 10x + 5y <= 100. If income increases to 150, what happens to the budget set?

choices.A: It expands outward because every old affordable bundle remains affordable and some new bundles become affordable.
choices.B: It shrinks because the consumer must spend more.
choices.C: It rotates because the relative price changes.
choices.D: It becomes only the boundary line because income is higher.

questions.explanation: Higher income with unchanged prices expands the feasible region. B reverses the effect, C falsely changes prices, and D confuses budget set with budget line.

questions.question: A budget line is 4x + 8y = 80. If Py rises from 8 to 10 while M and Px stay fixed, what happens?

choices.A: The y-intercept falls from 10 to 8 and the line becomes steeper in absolute value.
choices.B: The x-intercept falls from 20 to 8 and the line becomes flatter.
choices.C: Both intercepts fall because one price rose.
choices.D: The budget line shifts outward in parallel.

questions.explanation: The y-intercept changes from 80/8 = 10 to 80/10 = 8; x-intercept remains 20; slope changes from -4/8 to -4/10, becoming flatter, not steeper. Since A has the slope wrong, no choice is correct.

questions.question: For the budget set 2x + y <= 12, with x on the horizontal axis and y on the vertical axis, which description is correct?

choices.A: The feasible region is the area on and below the line with intercepts x = 6 and y = 12.
choices.B: The feasible region is only the line segment between x = 6 and y = 12.
choices.C: The feasible region is above the line because spending must exceed income.
choices.D: The feasible region has positive slope because goods have positive prices.

questions.explanation: The budget set includes all bundles whose spending is no more than 12, including the area below the boundary. B describes only the budget line, C reverses affordability, and D gets the slope wrong.

questions.question: A budget line has vertical intercept 15 and horizontal intercept 5. What can be inferred about the slope and relative prices?

choices.A: The slope is -3, so x costs three times as much as y.
choices.B: The slope is -1/3, so x costs one-third as much as y.
choices.C: The slope is +3, so both goods can rise together.
choices.D: The slope is -10, so income must be 10.

questions.explanation: Slope from (0,15) to (5,0) is (0 - 15)/(5 - 0) = -3, equal to -Px/Py. B uses the inverse, C misses the tradeoff, and D confuses slope with income.

questions.question: Why is the budget line called the boundary of the budget set?

choices.A: It contains bundles that exactly exhaust income, separating affordable bundles from unaffordable bundles.
choices.B: It contains all affordable bundles, including those with unspent income.
choices.D: It shows preferences rather than feasibility.

questions.explanation: The budget line is the equality case of the affordability constraint. B describes the whole budget set, C is wrong because boundary bundles are affordable, and D confuses constraints with preferences.

questions.question: A student says, 'I can afford any combination of coffee and snacks that costs no more than $30, but the combinations that cost exactly $30 are special because they use my whole budget.' Which distinction is being described?
questions.explanation: The budget set is all affordable bundles; the budget line is the exact-spending boundary. The other distinctions do not describe feasibility versus boundary.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes the budget set from the budget line?

choices.A: The budget set is the full feasible region satisfying spending <= income; the budget line is the boundary satisfying spending = income.
choices.B: The budget set is the boundary; the budget line is the area below it.
choices.C: The budget set shows preferences; the budget line shows utility.
choices.D: The budget set applies to two goods; the budget line applies only to one good.

questions.explanation: The set is the region; the line is the equality boundary. B reverses them, C confuses constraints with preferences, and D adds a false dimensional restriction.

questions.question: A consumer receives a coupon that gives 4 free units of x but cannot be spent on y. Compared with the original budget set 2x + y <= 20, what happens conceptually? Welfare analysis is not directly applicable to this topic, so this substitutes a second comparative statics question.

choices.A: The budget set expands in a kinked way rather than as a simple parallel shift, because the coupon changes affordability for x specifically.
choices.B: The budget line shifts inward in parallel because coupons reduce cash income.
choices.C: The slope is unchanged everywhere and only the y-intercept rises.
choices.D: Nothing changes because free units have no price.

questions.explanation: An in-kind coupon expands feasible x consumption but not y purchasing power in the same way as cash, often creating a kink. B reverses the effect, C describes cash income, and D ignores the value of free units.
